original_abstract: All plants absorb Na+ from their environment when grown under saline conditions. Once it enters the symplast, Na+ is distributed throughout various cells, tissues, and organs. While it can serve as a beneficial osmoticum to lower cellular water potential, it also carries the risk of severe toxicity. Precise control over Na+ fluxes is therefore vital for survival. Current research indicates that the distinction between salt-tolerant and salt-sensitive species does not stem from different types of transporters, but rather from the sophisticated regulation of Na+ uptake and internal movement. Recently, several regulatory mechanisms have been identified, involving signaling molecules such as Ca2+, cyclic nucleotides, reactive oxygen species, and hormones, as well as complex transcriptional and post-translational modifications. This review provides a comprehensive overview of intra- and intercellular Na+ transport and summarizes current understanding of how these pathways are modulated during the critical early stages of salt stress.
humanized_abstract: In salinized solutions all plants take up Na+ from the solution surrounding the roots. Once Na+ enters the symplast, it moves to several locations within the plant cells, tissues and organs. Sodium can act as an osmotically effective ion that decreases the water potential of the plant cell, as well as being toxic. Thus Na+ uptake and translocation has to be tightly regulated in salt-resistant plants. The present assessment of the salt-resistance/salt-sensitivity of plants to salt stress indicates that it is not associated with transport mechanisms that differ in any way from those present in salt-sensitive species, but with regulation of sodium influx and translocation within the plant. Increasing numbers of salt stress-regulatory mechanisms are being revealed including several possible signals such as calcium ions, cyclic nucleotides, ROS and plant hormones, which control different levels of gene expression as well as post-transcriptional regulation and modification of salt-stress-associated proteins. The purpose of this review is to summarize present knowledge of Na+ transport within plants under salt-stress conditions and the regulation of these processes, particularly in the crucial salt-stress acquisition phase of salt-stressed plants.
